coredata.dc:title: Determination of third order susceptibility of carbon quantum dots at different concentrations

coredata.dc:creator: Abdullah, L.A.
Ibrahim, O.A.
Ali, O.A.
coredata.dc:description: The nonlinear refractive (NLR) index and third order susceptiblity (X3) of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been studied using two laser wavelengths (473 and 532 nm). The z- scan technique was used to examine the nonlinearity. Ruslts showed that all concentrations have negative NLR indices in the order of 10−10 cm2/W at two laser wavelengths. Moreover, the nonlinearity of CQDs were improved by increasing the concentration of CQDs. The highest value of third order suscebtibility was found to be 3.32*10−8(esu) for CQDs with concentration 70 mA at 473 nm wavelength.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Third order susceptibility
Nonlinear refractive index
Carbon quantum dots
Z- scan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption curves of CQD at different concentrations.
Absorption coefficient of CQD at different concentrations.
Closed aperture distance of CQDs (a) at 473 nm wavelength (b) at 532 nm wavelength.
The nonlinear optical properties of CQD at different concentrations.

head.ce:title: Determination of third order susceptibility of carbon quantum dots at different concentrations

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics. College of Science, University of Baghdad, Baghdad, Iraq

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics. College of Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics. College of Science, University of Baghdad, Baghdad, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nonlinear refractive (NLR) index and third order susceptiblity (X3) of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been studied using two laser wavelengths (473 and 532 nm). The z- scan technique was used to examine the nonlinearity. Ruslts showed that all concentrations have negative NLR indices in the order of 10−10 cm2/W at two laser wavelengths. Moreover, the nonlinearity of CQDs were improved by increasing the concentration of CQDs. The highest value of third order suscebtibility was found to be 3.32*10−8(esu) for CQDs with concentration 70 mA at 473 nm wavelength.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs are a fascinating subset of carbon nanoparticles with a size of around 10 nm [1]. They not only overcomes specific limitations of typical organic dyes and nanomaterials, but also exhibits enhanced performance compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots. Such advantages include minimal molecular weight, reduced toxicity, an extensive of raw material sources, small particle dimensions, low cost, adjustable emission spectra, highly tunable photoluminescence behavior, and strong biocompatibility [2,3].

The strong nonlinear optical (NLO) properties exhibited by low-dimensional materials, like QDs have a significant attention topic in recent research [4]. The tunable emission characteristics of quantum dots through variations in their sizes, making them a potentially valuable in applications such as photo-electronics, nonlinear optics, the fabrication of light-emitting diodes, photovoltaic devices and laser technologies [5].

The third-order susceptibility is used to characterize the NLO properties of CQDs. These properties describe how the material behaves under high-intensity light [6]. The third-order susceptibility characterizes the material’s NLO behavior, such as the generation of harmonic frequencies and optical Kerr effects. Due to their unique electronic structure and size-dependent properties, nanoscale carbon structures, like Carbon quantum dots exhibit interesting nonlinear optical behavior [7].

Various techniques exist for measureing the non linear optical characteristics of materials, including nonlinear interferometry, degenerate four wave mixing, and Z- scan beam distortion analysis [8,9]. While the initial two approaches, interferometry and wave mixing offer high sensitivity potential but necessitate a complex experimental arrangement. Conversely, the Z-scan method utilizes the concept of spatial beam distortion caused by self-focusing or defocusing processes resulting from the combination effect of an intensity-dependent refractive index of the medium. This approach enables the determination of both the sign and magnitude of the refractive nonlinearities using a single beam, thereby providing simplicity and high sensitivity [10].

The aim of this paper is to determine the third order optical susceptibility of CQDs at various concentrations using two diode- pumped solid state continuous wave lasers operating at 473 and 532 nm wavelengths.

CQDs preparation method was described in a previous publications [11,12]. Ethanol solution of 99.5 ml was mixed with 0.5 ml of distilled water to reach a total volume of 1 L. Subsequently, a mass of 0.3 g of NaOH was dissolved in the ethanol solution over a duration of 3 h using a magnetic stirrer. Then, 100 ml of NaOH solution was poured into a 250 ml beaker for installing graphite rods with a separation of 1 in. between the carbon rods in a solution.

An electric current was adjusted to 10 mA and utilized for 3 h to ensure the movement of ions between electrodes, the electrodes were flipped every 5 min. The resulting mild milky color solution was then decanted into vials to label and close them. After a few days, the solutions turned into a yellow orangish color. This process was repeated with current of 10, 30, 50, and 70 mA.

The Z scan measurement method represents a simple experimental approach that gives information on the nonlinear optical properties of various materials. The Z scan setup was similar to that reported by H. A et al. [13]. In this method, the sample is scanned through a focused beam, recording the transmittance of the far field relative to the sample’s position. Two diode- pumped solid state CW lasers operating at 473 and 532 nm with beam sizes of 1.94 and 1.9 mm, respectively. By focusing the laser beam to a small spot using an 8.5 cm focal length convex lens, At the focus point the intensity of laser beam was calculated to be 1.588247 and 2.090526 mW/cm2 for the wavelengths 473 and 532 nm, respectively. The light transmitted through an aperture positioned in the far field is monitored based on the sample’s position. A motorized stage facilitated the movement of the sample and data were automatically recorded at 15 µm intervals. The normalized transmittance is given by the following [14]:

)), is the linear transmittance of the aperture, while Leff=(1-e−αL) is the sample effective thickness, and Io is the intensity of the incident light at a specific focal point [15].

In a closed aperture, if the z scan data exhibits a valley followed by a peak or vice versa, it indicates the presence of a self focusing or self defocusing material. The positive and negative nonlinear refractive indices are related to the behavior of the self focusing or self defocusing sample [16].

The determination of the real

parts of the third order nonlinear optical susceptibility, are calculated according to the relations [17],

Where

is a fundamental constant in physics denoted to the vacuum permittivity (8.85*10−12 F/m), c is the light velocity, and no is the linear refractive index of the sample. The obsolute value is computed using relations:

These parameters offer significant insights into the optical response of the material and are crucial for further analysis and interpretation.
The linear absorption of the samples has been obtained using UV– visible spectrophotometer (LAMBDA 365).

Fig. 1 is the absorption spectrum of a CQDs for different concentrations (10,30,50,70). The absorption spectrum reveals that the material has an absorption edge between (300–500) nm and it is transparent in the other region. These findings are in agreement with the literatures.

Fig. 1 also illustrates a significant peak at 380 nm corresponds to the CQDs. This absorption peak exhibits a red shift in comparison to the bulk exciton absorption of CQDs which occurs at 230 nm, attributed to the nanoscale dimensions of the structures.

The absorption coefficient of CQDs at different concentrations are found at two wavelengths 473 and 532 nm. Note that the linear absorption coefficient increased by increasing concentration of CQDs and the absorption coefficient at 473 nm is the largest. Fig. 2 shows the absorption coefficient of CQDs at different concentrations.

The nonlinear transmittance curves of the closed aperture z scan for CQD are revealed in Fig. 3 for two wavelengths (473 and 532 nm), respectively. The position of these laser wavelengths utilized in the research with respect to the absorption band of CQDs is depicted in Fig. 1. The normalized curves of the closed aperture Z-scan reveals a distinct peak and valley configuration indicative of a self-defocusing optical material. As the valley follows the peak in transmittance, signifying a negative refractive nonlinearity of CQD for all concentrations that it shows the self-defocusing effect of the synthesized samples. The n2 values are calculated using Eq. (1) and the NLR indices were in the order of 10−10 cm2/W that was listed in Table 1 . Also, the third order susceptibility was determined to be in the order of 10−8 (esu). It is obvious that by increasing concentration of CQDs, the amount of NLR and third order suscebtipility increased and this due to aggregation effects of nanoparticles when the concentration of CQDs increase. The highest recorded value of third order suscebtibility was 3.32*10−8(esu) at a concentration of 70 mA at 473 nm wavelength. Also, the optical nonlinearity is greater when utilizing a laser with a wavelength of 473 nm. In the present study, the CQDs do not show any nonlinear absorption behavior using the open aperture z scan experiment using a low power laser operating at 473 and 532 nm.

Carbon quantum dots were prepared by chemical method at different concentrations (10,30,50,70 mA). The optical absorption of CQD was investigated using UV–VIS spectroscopy. NLO properties were estimated using z scan technique. It has been determined that the nonlinear refractive index of CQDs displays a negative value and in order of 10−10 cm2/W. Findings showed that the NLR index and third order suscebyibility have maximum value at 473 nm. An increase in the NLR index and third order suscebtipility of samples with increasing concentration was observed. High NLR index arises from the aggregation effects of nanoparticles when the concentration of CQDs increase. It can be included that the prepared CQDs can be used in optoelectronic and nonlinear optical device applications.

L.A. Abdullah: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. O.A. Ibrahim: Data curation, Formal analysis, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. O.A. Ali: Data curation, Formal analysis, Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang and A. Hu, “ Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications,” Journal of Materials Chemistry C, 34, 2014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A mini review on carbon quantum dot: preparation, properties, and electrocatalytic application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, Y.Feng, P.Dong, J. Huang,“ A mini review on carbon quantum dot: preparation, properties, and electrocatalytic application,” Front. Chem. 7, 671, 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dota and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Y. Lim, W. Shen, Z. Gao, “Carbon quantum dota and their applications,” Chemical Society Review, 1,(2015).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nonlinear optical properties of CdSe and CdTe core-shell quantum dots and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kalsoom Ue, Yi R, Qu J and Liu L, “ Nonlinear Optical Properties of CdSe and CdTe Core-Shell Quantum Dots and Their Applications,” Front. Phys. 9:612070. (2021).
Prem Prabhakaran, Won Jin Kim, Kwang-Sup Lee, and Paras N.,“Quantum dots (QDs) for photonic applications,” Optical Materials Express Vol. 2, 2012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Third-order nonlinear optical response and optical limiting of colloidal carbon dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots with honeycomb structure: a novel synthesis approach utilizing cigarette smoke precursors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Setianto, S., Men, L.K., Bahtiar, A. et al. Carbon quantum dots with honeycomb structure: a novel synthesis approach utilizing cigarette smoke precursors. Sci Rep 14, 1996 (2024).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The third-order nonlinear optical susceptibility of the quantum dot as a three-level system in a hybrid structure

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Neda Biranvand and Ali Bahari, “The third-order nonlinear optical susceptibility of the quantum dot as a three-level system in a hybrid structure,” Journal of applied physics, Vol.133, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nonlinear optical glasses for ultrafast optical switches

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. R. Friberg and P. W. Smith, Nonlinear Optical Glasses for Ultrafast Optical Switches, IEEE J. Quantum Electron., Vol. 23, 1987, PP. 2089 – 2094.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.M. Jawad, L.A. Abdullah,“ Novel characteristics of CQDs synthesized by electrochemical method,” J Opt(2024).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical characterization of Methylene blue over the whole visible range

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.A.Majeed and A.B.Sharba,“ Optical characterization of Methylene blue over the whole visible range,” AIP conference Proceedings 2547, 030006 (2022).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nonlinear optical properties of pure and Ag/polyaniline nanocomposite thin films deposited by plasma jet

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. R. Humud,“ Nonlinear Optical Properties of Pure and Ag/Polyaniline Nanocomposite Thin Films Deposited by Plasma Jet,” Iraqi Journal of Science, vol. 57, no. 2C, pp. 1408–1414, Jun. 2022, Accessed: Apr. 18, 2024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non linear optical properties of silver nanoparticles

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Linear and nonlinear optical properties ofanthocyanin dye from red cabbage in different pH solutions

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The third-order nonlinear optical susceptibility of gold

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. W. Boyd, Z. Shi, and I. D. Leon,“ The third- order nonlinear optical susceptibility of gold,” Optics Communications, 2014.
